title: Linear Regression from Scratch
excerpt: Most machine learning courses hand you sklearn and tell you to call .fit(). That is fine for getting things done fast, but it skips the part that actually matters: understanding what the algorithm is doing underneath. In this post, I build linear regression from the ground up using only NumPy forward pass, loss function, gradients, weight updates, all of it written by hand.
content:
Why Build It from Scratch?

There is a version of learning machine learning that goes like this: import sklearn, call LinearRegression().fit(X, y), check the score, move on. You can build a lot of things that way. But there is a gap between being able to use a tool and actually understanding it — and that gap tends to show up at the worst possible times.

When I started this project, my goal was simple: understand what really happens inside a linear regression model. Not the abstraction. The actual math, turned into actual code, running step by step. No sklearn. No autograd. No magic.

What came out was a clean NumPy implementation that computes predictions, measures loss, calculates gradients by hand using the chain rule, and updates weights using gradient descent. The whole thing is about 30 lines of real logic.

This post is a walkthrough of exactly how it works.

Repository: github.com/smtkanchana66/Linear-Regression-from-Scratch NumPy only, fully implemented in a Jupyter notebook.

What Is Linear Regression, Really?

Linear regression is the simplest supervised learning algorithm. Given a set of input features x, it tries to predict a continuous output y by learning a linear relationship between them.

The core idea: find a line (or hyperplane, in multiple dimensions) that best fits your data. That line is defined by weights w (one per feature) and a bias b. The model makes predictions like this:

y_hat = w · x + b

Where · is the dot product, multiply each feature value by its corresponding weight and sum everything up, then add the bias. That is your prediction.

The job of training is to find the values of w and b that make y_hat as close as possible to the true y across all your training examples. We measure closeness with a loss function, and we improve iteratively using gradient descent.

The Math Before the Code

Let me lay out the math clearly before we touch any code. There are four equations you need to understand. Everything else follows from these.

1. The Prediction

y_hat = w · x + b

For a single sample with 4 features, this is:

y_hat = w[0]*x[0] + w[1]*x[1] + w[2]*x[2] + w[3]*x[3] + b

For a whole batch of n samples, we use a matrix multiply NumPy makes this one line.

2. The Loss Function - Mean Squared Error (MSE)

MSE measures how wrong our predictions are on average. We take the difference between each prediction and the real answer, square it (so negatives don't cancel positives), and average across all samples:

J = (1/n) * sum((y_hat - y) ** 2)

Why square it? Two reasons: it penalizes large errors more than small ones, and it makes the math for the gradient much cleaner.

3. The Gradients - How to Improve

Gradient descent needs to know: if I nudge each weight slightly, does the loss go up or down? That is what the gradient tells us. We compute it using the chain rule:

# Gradient with respect to weights
dJ_dw = (2/n) * sum((y_hat - y) * x)

# Gradient with respect to bias
dJ_db = (2/n) * sum(y_hat - y)

The 2/n comes from differentiating the squared error. The x factor in the weight gradient comes from the chain rule the prediction depends on w through w · x, so when we differentiate through the prediction, x comes along.

4. The Weight Update

Once we have the gradients, we move the weights in the opposite direction — down the slope toward lower loss:

w = w - learning_rate * dJ_dw
b = b - learning_rate * dJ_db

The learning_rate (often written as lr or α) controls the step size. Too large and you overshoot. Too small and training takes forever.

These four equations - prediction, loss, gradients, update are the entire algorithm. Every iteration of training just runs through them in order. That is gradient descent.

The Implementation

Now let us turn that math into code. The full implementation lives in Linear_regression.ipynb. Here it is broken down piece by piece.

Step 1: Imports

import numpy as np

That is the only import. No sklearn. No autograd. No matplotlib even (though you would add that in practice for the cost curve). Just NumPy.

Step 2: The Prediction Function

def pred_vec(X, w, b):
    return np.dot(X, w) + b

This is the forward pass. np.dot(X, w) performs a matrix-vector multiply: for each row of X (one training sample), it computes the dot product with w. Adding b broadcasts the bias across all predictions. The result is a vector of predictions, one per sample.

Important: your input array must be dtype=float. If you pass integer arrays, NumPy will silently do integer math and your gradients will be wrong. This is one of those bugs that is painful to track down the first time you hit it.

Step 3: The Loss Function

def mse_loss(y_true, y_pred):
    n = len(y_true)
    return (1/n) * np.sum((y_pred - y_true) ** 2)

Clean and direct. (y_pred - y_true) ** 2 computes the squared error for every sample as a vector, and np.sum() adds them all up. We divide by n to get the average.

Step 4: The Gradient Descent Update

def gra(y_true, y_pred, learning_rate, X_train, w, b):
    n = len(y_true)
    error = y_pred - y_true                        # shape: (n,)

    dw = (2/n) * np.dot(X_train.T, error)          # shape: (features,)
    db = (2/n) * np.sum(error)                     # scalar

    w = w - learning_rate * dw
    b = b - learning_rate * db

    return w, b

This is where the real learning happens. Let us walk through it:

  • error = y_pred - y_true  the residuals for each sample. This is how wrong we were, with sign.

  • np.dot(X_train.T, error)  this is the vectorized form of (2/n) * Σ error_i * x_i. Transposing X_train turns each feature into a row, so the dot product gives us one gradient value per weight.

  • np.sum(error) the bias gradient is just the sum of all residuals.

  • The final two lines apply the update rule, moving weights in the direction that reduces loss.

Step 5: The Training Loop

X_train = np.array([
    [1, 5, 2, 8],
    [2, 3, 1, 6],
    [3, 7, 4, 2],
    [4, 1, 2, 9],
    [5, 4, 3, 1],
], dtype=float)

# True relationship: y = 2*x1 + 3*x3
y_train = np.array([8, 7, 14, 14, 19], dtype=float)

# Initialize parameters to zero
w    = np.zeros(4)
bias = 0.0

cost_history = []

for epoch in range(1000):
    predictions = pred_vec(X_train, w, bias)
    cost        = mse_loss(y_train, predictions)
    cost_history.append(cost)
    w, bias     = gra(y_train, predictions, learning_rate=0.01,
                       X_train=X_train, w=w, b=bias)

print(f"Final weights: {w}")
print(f"Final bias:    {bias:.4f}")
print(f"Final cost:    {cost_history[-1]:.6f}")

Each epoch does exactly three things: predict, measure loss, update. After 1000 epochs, the weights converge.

Results: Does It Actually Work?

The training data was constructed with a known relationship: y = 2*x1 + 3*x3. This means the true weights are [2, 0, 3, 0] and the bias should be 0. Features x2 and x4 are noise they should be driven to zero.

After 1000 epochs at a learning rate of 0.01, the model outputs:

Final weights: [2.  0.  3.  0.]   # exactly right
Final bias:    0.0000             # exactly right
Final cost:    0.000000           # perfect fit

The algorithm correctly identified that only x1 and x3 matter and learned exactly the right coefficients. The irrelevant features were pushed to zero. This is gradient descent working exactly as it should.

This works cleanly because the data is noise-free and the true relationship is perfectly linear. In real datasets, you would expect small residual errors due to noise, and gradient descent would minimize but not eliminate the loss.

Understanding Gradient Descent Visually

It helps to have a mental picture of what gradient descent is actually doing. Imagine the loss function as a landscape a surface over the space of all possible weights. Every point on that surface is a particular set of weights and the corresponding loss value.

At the start, we place ourselves somewhere on that surface (at w = 0 in our case). The gradient tells us the slope at our current position which direction is uphill. We take a small step in the opposite direction (downhill). Then we recalculate, and step again. And again. After many steps, we end up at the bottom of a valley the minimum loss.

The learning rate controls how big each step is. With the wrong learning rate, the algorithm either:

  • Diverges learning rate too high, we overshoot the minimum and the loss explodes instead of decreasing

  • Converges very slowly learning rate too low, we take tiny steps and need many thousands of epochs

  • Converges nicely learning rate just right, the cost curve drops smoothly and levels off

For this project, lr = 0.01 worked well. In practice, finding the right learning rate is part of the craft of ML engineering.

Try adding print(f"Epoch {epoch}: cost = {cost:.4f}") every 100 epochs. You will see the cost drop fast at first, then slow as it approaches the minimum. That curve is gradient descent doing its job.

The Chain Rule: Why the Gradient Is What It Is

If you have not seen calculus in a while, the gradient formula might look like it appeared from nowhere. Let me show you exactly where it comes from.

We want to find dJ/dw how much does the loss change when we change a weight? The loss is J = (1/n) * Σ (y_hat - y)². The prediction is y_hat = w · x + b. So the loss depends on w through y_hat.

Using the chain rule:

dJ/dw = dJ/d(y_hat) * d(y_hat)/dw

# Step 1: differentiate the loss with respect to y_hat
# J = (1/n) * (y_hat - y)^2
# dJ/d(y_hat) = (2/n) * (y_hat - y)

# Step 2: differentiate y_hat with respect to w
# y_hat = w * x + b
# d(y_hat)/dw = x

# Multiply together:
# dJ/dw = (2/n) * (y_hat - y) * x

That is exactly the formula we used. The 2/n is the derivative of the squared error. The x comes from the derivative of the linear prediction. No mystery just calculus.

For the bias, the same logic applies but d(y_hat)/db = 1 (the bias adds directly, with no multiplier), so the gradient simplifies to just (2/n) * Σ (y_hat - y).

Key Things I Learned Building This

1. dtype=float is not optional

NumPy arrays default to integer dtype if you pass integer values. Integer division truncates, so gradients computed on integer arrays will be subtly wrong often zero when they should not be. Always declare dtype=float when creating training data arrays.

2. The transpose trick for vectorized gradients

The weight gradient formula is dJ/dw = (2/n) * Σ error_i * x_i, which is a sum over all samples. Writing this as a loop works, but np.dot(X_train.T, error) does the same thing in one vectorized operation it is both faster and cleaner. Understanding why it works (matrix multiplication is a sum of outer products) is a valuable NumPy mental model.

3. Zero initialization is fine for linear regression

Starting all weights at zero works here because linear regression has a convex loss surface there is only one minimum, and gradient descent will find it regardless of starting point. This is not true for neural networks, where zero initialization causes symmetry problems. Good to understand why the rule is different.

4. Feature scaling matters in the real world

In this synthetic dataset, all features are on a similar scale (roughly 1–9). If one feature were in the thousands and another in the range 0–1, gradient descent would behave very differently for each weight some gradients huge, some tiny. In practice, you normalize your features first. This project gives you a clean environment to understand the algorithm before that complexity enters.

5. Convergence is visible in the cost curve

Tracking cost_history and plotting it is not just nice to have it tells you whether your learning rate is right, whether you need more epochs, and whether something is broken. The cost should decrease monotonically (every epoch lower than the last). If it bounces or increases, your learning rate is too high.

How to Run It Yourself

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/smtkanchana66/Linear-Regression-from-Scratch.git
cd Linear-Regression-from-Scratch

# Install the only dependency
pip install numpy

# Open the notebook
jupyter notebook Linear_regression.ipynb

The notebook walks through everything: imports, function definitions, training data, the training loop, and results. You can change the learning rate, number of epochs, or the training data and watch how the convergence behavior changes. That kind of hands-on experimentation is where the real understanding comes from.

Where This Fits in the Bigger Picture

Linear regression is often described as the simplest ML algorithm, and that is true but it is also the foundation that everything else builds on. Logistic regression is linear regression with a sigmoid applied to the output. A neural network is many linear transformations stacked together with non-linearities between them. Even the attention mechanism in transformers involves weighted dot products that have their roots in the same math.

More importantly, gradient descent the optimizer you just implemented by hand is the training algorithm for virtually every deep learning model in existence today. The version used for training GPT or a ResNet is more sophisticated (Adam optimizer, learning rate schedules, mini-batches), but the core loop is identical: predict, compute loss, compute gradients, update weights.

When you understand that loop at this level when you have written the gradient by hand and watched the cost curve drop the black boxes of modern ML start to feel a lot less black.

The best way to understand a tool is to build it. After this, when you call sklearn's LinearRegression().fit(), you will know exactly what is happening inside. That knowledge compounds.

What Is Next

This implementation is intentionally minimal the goal was clarity, not completeness. Natural next steps if you want to extend it:

  • Plot the cost curve - add matplotlib and visualize cost_history over epochs. A healthy training run looks like a smooth exponential decay.

  • Add feature normalization - standardize your features to zero mean and unit variance before training. See how it affects convergence speed on unscaled data.

  • Try different learning rates - set lr = 1.0 and watch the loss explode. Set lr = 0.0001 and watch it barely move after 1000 epochs. The learning rate is one of the most important hyperparameters in all of ML.

  • Add mini-batch gradient descent - instead of computing gradients over the full dataset each epoch, split into batches of size 32 or 64. This is how real training works at scale.

  • Implement logistic regression - wrap the output in a sigmoid and swap MSE for binary cross-entropy. You have just extended this to a classifier.

  • Add L2 regularization (Ridge) - add a penalty term λ * ||w||² to the loss. Derive the new gradient (it is only one extra term) and implement it. Regularization is essential for real-world models.

Each of these extensions builds directly on what is already here. Start with the cost curve plot — it will teach you more about gradient descent than any lecture.

The full code is on GitHub: github.com/smtkanchana66/Linear-Regression-from-Scratch

Built from scratch as a learning exercise to understand the internals of linear regression. no sklearn, no autograd, no shortcuts. If you find it useful, feel free to fork it and extend it.
